segments.s753-g33: This is a prepositional/locative phrase with an agent and a continuous-past verb: ز (from) + هر سو (every direction/side) sets the origin/area; همی‌جست is an archaic imperfective past (همی‑ + جَسْت) meaning 'kept seeking' or 'was continually seeking'; بدخواه شاه is a genitival phrase 'the king's ill-wisher' (بدخواه = ill-wisher, شاه = king). Word order (preposition — area — verb — agent phrase) is poetic but means 'from every side the king's enemy kept searching.'
segments.s753-g34: چنین here is a discourse adverb 'thus/so' linking to the previous clause; تا introduces a temporal clause meaning 'until/when' (not purposive here). بدیدند is past plural 'they saw' and is the main verb of the subordinate clause; مردی is an indefinite noun 'a man'; به راه is a locative phrase 'on/toward the road' with به used in the spatial sense. The whole clause reads 'thus, when/and they saw a man on the road.'
segments.s753-g35: This is a nominal/descriptive clause without an explicit copula (typical in Persian poetry): دو چشمش 'his two eyes' is the subject, کبود is a predicative adjective 'dark-blue/azure.' و links the next predication: در خساره 'in (or from) loss/privation' sets the cause or condition, and زرد is the adjective 'pale/yellowish.' Together: 'his two eyes were dark blue, and (from privation) he was pale.'
segments.s753-g36: Another stacked nominal description: تنی خشک 'a body dry/gaunt' (تنی = bodily/one's body; خشک = gaunt/dry), و پر موی 'and full of hair' uses پر + noun = 'full of (hair),' و links to رخ لاژورد where رخ 'face' is qualified by لاژورد (lapis-lazuli/ultramarine), i.e. 'a lapis-blue face.' Adjectives follow the nouns in this poetic order.
segments.s753-g37: پر از + noun means 'full of (something)': پر از خاک پای literally 'full of the dust of the feet' (i.e. dusty feet). و joins the next nominal phrase شکم گرسنه 'a hungry belly' (شکم = stomach/belly, گرسنه = hungry). This line strings descriptive noun phrases without an explicit copula: '(he had) feet full of dust and a hungry belly.'
segments.s753-g38: تن مرد بیدادگر برهنه is a compact noun phrase: تن مرد 'the body/person of the man', بیدادگر is an attributive adjective/noun 'tyrant/oppressor' describing مرد, and برهنه 'naked' is the final predicate/adjective. In poetry the predicate can follow the noun phrase, yielding 'the (tyrannical) man's body was naked.'
segments.s753-g39: ندانست is the negative past form 'did not know' (here 'no one knew'); کس after a negated verb functions as 'anyone/no one' (context gives 'no one'); نام او 'his name' is the object, and در جهان 'in the world' is the locative phrase. The literal sense: 'No one knew his name in the world.'
segments.s753-g40: This is a poetic binary locution with میان repeated for emphasis: میان کهان و میان مهان uses میان 'between/among' twice to frame two spheres. کهان here refers to the heavens or the cosmic realm, مهان literally 'guests' (used here as a class/group), so the phrase intensifies 'in neither the heavens nor among the guests/people.' Grammatically it is a paired prepositional phrase expanding 'in the world' above.
segments.s753-g89: This clause begins a conditional with اگر (if). گنج داری is a simple present/near-future possession construction (گنج + داری = 'you have/possess'), addressed to the subject. و‌گر (an alternate conditional 'or if / but if') introduces a second condition; گرم is an adjectival state ('warm/fervent/zealous') describing the same subject, and the coordinating و links گرم to the noun رنج ('hardship/suffering'), which continues into the next segment.
segments.s753-g90: نمانی is the verb from ماندن with a prefixed ن- here giving a negative sense (classical/poetic word order places the verb after particles); it functions as 'you/one will not remain' or 'do(es) not remain'. همی is the older continuous/habitual particle often used with present/future verbs. در is the preposition 'in', followed by سرای (house/place) and سپنج (literally 'sponge' — here a compound name); note: the listed senses for سرای in the metadata do not include 'house', so the precise lexical index below is set to -1.
segments.s753-g91: This is an imperative: برگزین is the command 'choose/select'. بی‌آزاری ('harmlessness' / 'being inoffensive') and راستی ('truth/rectitude; honesty') are coordinated objects of the imperative (bi- prefix + آزار = 'without harm', راستی = uprightness), with و linking them.
segments.s753-g92: چو functions here as a temporal/subordinating conjunction 'when'. خواهی is the present/future form of خواستن ('you wish' / 'you want'). که introduces the subordinate clause whose verb is یابی ('you find/attain'); یابی is a present/subjunctive/indicative form meaning 'you obtain/attain'. به is the preposition 'by/through/from' and داد is used as the substantive 'Justice' (or 'the Just One'); آفرین in context likely refers to the Creator/praiser, but the sense-choices supplied in the metadata don't include 'Creator', so its index below is -1.
segments.s753-g93: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی (= news/information) is the subject that آمد ('came/reached'). به marks the direction 'to/into' and links to two coordinated places: بازار (bazaar/market) and راه (road/street), joined by و.
segments.s753-g94: که introduces a content clause explaining the news. خسرو is the subject 'the king' (here a title/name). بران گونه is a small adverbial phrase: بران ('on/thereupon/thereof') + گونه ('manner/way') = 'in that manner' or 'thus'. برشد is a past/perfect (poetic) form 'rose/was raised' and تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/corrupt'; syntactically the clause says the king rose (or acted) in that way and (was) ruined.
segments.s753-g95: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone'. بدگمانان is a plural substantive 'the suspicious/distrustful ones'. به + زندان is the prepositional phrase 'into prison'. شدند is the past/passive auxiliary 'became/were made' used here to mean 'were put/ended up', so the clause means 'all the suspicious were put into prison.'
segments.s753-g96: Another result clause with the same verbal form شدند ('were placed/ became'). به ایوان آن is a locative phrase 'into that iwan/veranda'. مستمندان is the plural noun 'the needy/poor', the logical patient of شوند; together: 'the needy were placed in that iwan.'
segments.s753-g57: مرا (object marker + pronoun fronted) + مهرهرمزد (proper name) + خوانند (archaic/poetic present plural) forms an impersonal present 'they call me Mehr‑Hormoz.' The following گفت is the reporting verb 'he said,' placed after the quoted content as a narrative tag.
segments.s753-g58: غریبم is the 1st‑person copula + adjective 'I am a stranger/foreigner.' بدین شهر is the prepositional phrase 'in this city' made of the prepositional prefix/particle ب + (here phonologically دین = این) + شهر. بی‌یار و جفت lists two negatives: 'without companion' and 'without a mate/partner.'
segments.s753-g59: چنین گفت خسرو is 'thus said Khosrow' (چنین = thus/so; گفت used as the past reporting verb). که here introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that' (temporal/conjunctive), followed by آمد زمان 'the time came.' Word order is typical poetical inversion: reporting clause then the temporal clause.
segments.s753-g60: بدست is the prepositional phrase 'into the hands / into the power (of).' فرومایه is an adjective 'base, lowly, vile' qualifying an implicit person. بدگمان is another adjective 'mistrustful / suspicious' — together they describe whose hands/time fell into.
segments.s753-g61: به مردم نماند is 'did not remain among the people' (به = among/for, مردم = the people, نماند = past negative 'did not remain'). همی‌چهراو is an emphatic archaic phrasing 'that very face' (همی + چهرا + او) serving as the subject/topic whose remaining is denied.
segments.s753-g62: به گیتی = 'in the world'; نجوید here means 'does not seek / does not desire' (poetic present used generically); کسی in this negative context functions as 'no one'; مهر او = 'his love/affection' is the object of desire. The whole clause: 'in the world no one seeks his love.'
segments.s753-g63: یکی ریدکی = 'a certain/one insignificant (man)' (یکی = a certain, ریدکی = derogatory 'lowly/insignificant fellow'). پیش او = 'before him / in his presence.' بد بپای is a verbal phrase meaning 'lay in ambush' or 'stood lying in wait' (بد qualifies the posture/intent as bad; بپای = to lie in wait).
segments.s753-g64: بریدک (a disparaging epithet/nickname for the man) + چنین گفت = 'thus said (he)' — گفت is the past reporting verb. کای is the vocative 'O,' and رهنمای is the noun 'guide/advisor.' The segment introduces the rogue's direct address to the guide.
segments.s753-g9: This is a sequence of prepositional phrases with a fronted adverb: ز درگاه = 'from the court' (ز is the poetic from), یکسر = 'entirely/at once' modifying the motion, and به نزدِ قباد = 'to the presence of Qobad' (the double preposition به نزد is a literary formula meaning 'to X's presence'). Word order is slightly inverted for poetic rhythm but functions as a single motion phrase.
segments.s753-g10: از آن کار بیداد کردند یاد is a poetic inversion of the verb phrase 'یاد کردن از ...' meaning 'to make mention of / to speak of'. از آن کار is the object ('of that deed'), بیداد qualifies کار ('that injustice/deed of oppression'), کردند is the past plural verb 'they did/said', and یاد is the nominal 'mention/recall' placed after the verb for emphasis — the whole means 'they mentioned/recalled that injustice'.
segments.s753-g11: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). یک‌بار گفتیم = 'we said it once' (گفتیم is past 'we said'); و این دیگرست = 'and this is different/another' (دیگرست is an archaic poetic copula meaning 'is different / is otherwise'). The clause states a previous utterance and contrasts it with the present matter.
segments.s753-g12: تو را is topicalized object/target marker ('as for you'); خود is an intensifier/reflective 'itself/indeed'; جزین = 'except this' (جز + این compressed in poetry); داوری درسرست = 'judgment is in the mind' (در سر است = 'is in [his] head/mind'). Together: 'for you, there is no judgment in his mind except this.' Word order is compressed and literary, with درسرست fused as a predicate.
segments.s753-g13: نشسته is a participial/predicative form 'seated' or 'dwelling'; به یک شهر is the locative 'in one city'. Poetic inversion places the qualifying adjective بی‌بر ('without offspring') before or adjacent to the kings: syntactically this reads as 'دو شاه بی‌بر' = 'two kings without heirs' seated in one city. Persian poetic word order often moves modifiers for rhythm, so interpret بی‌بر as qualifying the kings.
segments.s753-g14: This is a balanced couplet: یکی ... یکی ... = 'one ... the other ...'. گاه normally means 'time/occasion' but here functions idiomatically as 'place/seat/station' (poetic usage), دارد = 'has/possesses', and زیرگاه = 'low station/under-place'. So the sense is 'one had the higher station, the other the lower.' The verbs are implied for the second half by repetition.
segments.s753-g15: چو = 'when'; خویشی فزاید is a clause with the present/future-form verb فزاید 'increases' used as a general or habitual condition (classical usage often uses present for general truths); پدر با پسر = 'father with son' (the parties between whom kinship grows). The whole subordinate clause sets the temporal/conditional background: 'When kinship increases between father and son...'
segments.s753-g16: همه بندگان را ببرند سر is a compact, forceful phrase: همه بندگان را = 'all the servants/subjects' with the object marker را; ببرند is a 3rd person plural (subjunctive/epic present) of بردن 'to take / to cut off', here idiomatically meaning 'cut off' (heads); سر = 'head' follows the verb, giving the resulting action 'they cut off the heads of all the servants.' The verb form is narrative/poetic rather than ordinary present.
segments.s753-g65: A compact list of offerings introduced without an overt verb here; the first item is a basin (implied) containing water and scents. The repeated و links the nouns 'water', 'musk', and 'عبیر' (fragrance/ambergris); بروتشت is an archaic/fused form (see note) functioning to place or present the basin before someone.
segments.s753-g66: A noun phrase describing a single garment: یکی is the indefinite 'a/certain', پاک is an adjective 'pure/clean' modifying the following noun, ترجامه (here a noun for a garment/covering in context) is the head noun, and دلپذیر is a postpositive adjective 'pleasing to the heart' qualifying that noun. Word order (indefinite + adjective + noun + adjective) is a poetic, compact listing.
segments.s753-g67: Simple past clause: پرستنده (the worshipper/devoted listener) is the subject; بشنید is the past-tense verb 'heard'; آواز is the direct object 'voice'; اوی is the enclitic possessive 'his/her', so آواز اوی = 'his voice'. The order Subject–Verb–Object is normal in older Persian narrative.
segments.s753-g68: Negative past clause: ندانست is the past negative (did not know/understand); کودك is the subject 'the child' placed after the verb (permissible in poetry); همی is an archaic particle here meaning 'still / as yet' or emphatic 'indeed'; راز اوی is the object 'his secret' with اوی as possessive. Overall: 'the child still did not know his secret.'
segments.s753-g69: Locative/motion clause: ز + پیشش ('from before him' / 'approaching his presence') introduces the motion, بیامد is the past tense 'came', and پرستار خرد is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the nurse of the little one' (خرد here functioning as 'the small/child'). The line therefore means 'the child's nurse came forward.'
segments.s753-g70: Another compact clause: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (here 'a'); تشت زرین is the noun phrase 'golden basin' (تشت head noun + زرین adjective); بر شاه برد is an archaic order for 'brought to the king' where برد is the past-tense verb 'brought' and بر شاه marks the recipient/location 'to/ before the king'.
segments.s753-g71: A prepositional/listing phrase: the first word (ابا) is an archaic/fused prepositional element introducing the accompanying items (here 'with'); جامه 'robe' is first, then و links the items, آبدستان is the water‑attendant/cupbearer, and وآب adds 'and water'. The whole phrase lists what accompanies the action in the next segment.
segments.s753-g72: Verb phrase with an archaic imperfective/periphrastic form: همی‌کرد is an old progressive/past habitual 'was doing / made' used here to form 'made haste'; خسرو is the subject (the king); ببردن is the infinitive 'to carry / to take' and شتاب ('haste') functions adverbially — together meaning 'Khosrow hurried to have them carried' or 'Khosrow hastened to take them.'
segments.s753-g105: بدان تا is an archaic/poetic connective meaning “to that (end), so that.” Here the initial ب (بـ-) is the prepositional/prefix sense “to/for” attached to دان (an old/contracted form meaning ‘that’/‘to that’), and تا introduces the purpose/result clause (“so that”). زن و کودکانشان is a coordinated noun phrase “wives and their children” (کودکان + شان = their children). نگاه here is the nominal element of the compound verb نگاه داشتن (here split across the clause), meaning “keeping/looking after” and awaits the verb in the next segment.
segments.s753-g106: بدارد is the third-person singular subjunctive/optative form of داشتن used with نگاه to make the verbal compound نگاه بدارد = “may/should keep, look after.” پس از is the fixed postpositional phrase meaning “after” (پس + از = after), followed by مرگ آن کشته شاه “the death of that slain king.” Word order is typical poetic: object/time follows the verb phrase.
segments.s753-g107: شد is the past/becoming verb meaning “became” or “came into being.” آن پادشاهی is the noun phrase “that kingship/kingdom.” و چندان سپاه links with the next segment: چندان here means “to that extent / so great,” and سپاه = “army/host.” The clause states existence/coming into being in a compact, poetic order.
segments.s753-g108: This continues the list describing the scale of that kingship: بزرگی here denotes “greatness/grandeur,” و مردی lists “men” (or manly warriors) as an additional element, and آن دستگاه = “that court/establishment.” The coordination with و links qualities (greatness) and constituents (army, men, court).
segments.s753-g109: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (“such that / namely that”). کس را here with را (object/definiteness marker) and following نبود yields the meaning “there was no one” (کس used negatively = “no one”). ز + شاهنشهان = “from/among the shahanshahs,” and آن is a demonstrative ‘those.’ نبود is the past negative of بودن, “was not / there was not.”
segments.s753-g110: نه ... شنود forms the negative continuation: نه از نامداران پیشین شنود = “nor was (he) heard of among the renowned of old.” از here means “among/of” in the sense “known among,” نامداران پیشین = “renowned/illustrious ones of former times,” and شنود is an archaic third-person form meaning “is/heard” (here functioning in the narrative to mean ‘was heard of’).
segments.s753-g111: یکی گشت = “one became / someone turned into” (یکی = a certain person; گشت = past of گشتن used as ‘became’). با آنک is a conjunctive phrase (با + آنک) meaning “with that (fact) that / so that,” introducing the result clause. نانی فراخ = “ample bread” (نانی = bread/morsel, فراخ = plentiful/ample).
segments.s753-g112: نیابد and نبیند are negative forms (subjunctive/preterital in classical style) meaning “did not find” and “did not see” respectively; together they state the result: he could not find bread and did not see (i.e. had no) home or palace. برو here is a brief poetic item preceding بوم و کاخ (بوم = dwelling/home, کاخ = palace); in this word order the verbs govern the nouns that follow, giving the sense “he saw neither home nor palace.”
segments.s753-g113: خردمند is the subject 'the wise (man)'. گوید is a literary present-tense reporting verb 'says' that introduces a quoted or reported clause. نیارد بها is an older/poetic verbal phrase: نیارد is the negative/archaic verbal form meaning 'does not bring/obtain' (here used predicatively) and بها is the object 'value/price', so together they mean 'brings/gets no value' or 'has no worth'.
segments.s753-g114: هر آنکس که is a universal relative: هر 'every/any', آنکس 'that person/one', and که introduces the relative clause 'who/that'. ایمن شد is a past/resultative clause: ایمن 'safe' + شد past of شدن 'became/was made', meaning 'who became/was made safe'. از marks source/agent 'from', and اژدها is the agent 'the dragon'. The whole segment is the subject of the preceding predicate (i.e. 'whoever became safe from the dragon').
segments.s753-g115: جهان رامخوان is a compound notion '(one who) tames the world' (جهان = world, رامخوان = tamer/charmer). جز introduces an exception 'except', and دلاور نهنگ is the exception: دلاور 'the brave/valiant (one)' qualified metaphorically by نهنگ 'the great/huge (lit. whale)' meaning a mighty hero. The phrase asserts that only such a mighty brave man can 'tame the world'.
segments.s753-g116: This clause describes the manner of the mighty one's action. بخاید here is a poetic/archaic verbal form expressing desire/effort (the supplied lemma's senses don't match modern usage, so the exact morphological pedigree is irregular in the gloss). به دندان 'with (his) tooth/fang' gives the instrument, چو (here) = 'when' or 'as' introducing the accompanying action, گیرد is present/future third-person 'seizes/grasps', به چنگ 'with his claw/hand' complements گیرد. Together: 'he desires/acts with his tooth when he seizes with his claw' — idiomatically, 'he will bite when he grabs'.
segments.s753-g117: سرآمد here means 'the end/consummation' or 'was completed'. کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. کار پرویز شاه is the noun phrase 'the work/enterprise of Parviz the king' (کار = work/affair, پرویز = the king's name, شاه = king/title). Word order is literary: the conclusion verb phrase precedes the noun phrase it qualifies in classical style, yielding 'Now the work of King Parviz has reached its end.'
segments.s753-g118: شد is the past/preterite of شدن 'became/was made', linking the completion to what exists now. آن نامور is 'that famed/renowned', functioning as a determiner + adjective for the following list: تخت 'throne', و 'and', گنج 'treasure', و 'and', سپاه 'army'. The whole means 'that renowned throne, treasure, and army came into being / were established.'
segments.s753-g25: به + جویید: the preposition/particle به precedes the verb to give a directional or exhortative sense ('go, do X'); جویید is the classical present-subjunctive/imperative plural (from جستن/جوییدن) meaning 'seek' or 'search for'. تا introduces a result/purpose clause 'تا کیست اندر جهان' (= 'to find who is in the world'). کیست is the interrogative pronoun کی with the copula ست (postposed copula common in classical Persian) meaning 'who is', and اندر جهان is a locative postpositional phrase 'in the world' modifying کی.
segments.s753-g26: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that/who'); این رنج is the direct object 'this suffering'; بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'upon us'; سرآرد is a classical present/subjunctive/optative third-person form meaning 'brings about' or 'befalls' (here active — 'brings upon'); نهان is adverbial here 'secretly' and sits after the verb in typical poetic order.
segments.s753-g27: Here the phrase is inverted for emphasis: کشنده ('a killer') is fronted as the object/theme, همی‌جست is the imperfective/progressive classical form meaning 'kept seeking' (the prefix همی- marks ongoing action), and بدخواه شاه is the agent ('the king's ill-wisher' — بدخواه governing شاه). In normal prose order the subject (بدخواه شاه) would precede the verb.
segments.s753-g28: ب + دان here function together as بدان 'with that / thereupon' (the tokenization shows the prefix separately), تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that'; کنندش is the third-person plural subjunctive/optative کنَند plus the enclitic -ش 'him/it' meaning 'they might do him/ it'; نهانی is adverbial 'secretly'; تباه is the result adjective 'ruined/destroyed' — so the clause means 'so that they may secretly ruin him.'
segments.s753-g29: کس اندر جهان is a standard subject phrase 'no one in the world' (کس here indefinite/negative). زهره آن is the object phrase 'that Zohreh' (Zohreh/Venus used metaphorically for radiance/beauty); نداشت is the past negative of داشتن 'did not have/possess'. Word order is subject – locative – object – predicate (standard simple clause with past negation).
segments.s753-g30: زمردی همان بهره آن نداشت parallels the previous clause: زمردی 'emerald(s)' (here the gem sense), همان بهره 'that same share/portion' and آن again 'that', followed by نداشت 'did not have'. The clause repeats structure for emphasis — nobody possessed that identical portion of emeralds.
segments.s753-g31: که introduces the descriptive/subordinate clause 'that'; خون is the direct object 'blood'; چنان 'such/so' modifies the following adjective; خسروی is an adjective 'kingly/royal' qualifying the manner or quality of the blood; ریختی is a classical past form of ریختن 'poured/ shed' (here perfective/past), giving 'shed/spilled such kingly blood.'
segments.s753-g32: همی‌کوه ('every/whole mountain(s)') is fronted as the (figurative) object; در گردن is a locative phrase 'on/around the neck'; آویختی is the past tense of آویختن 'hung' — together: 'he hung whole mountains about his neck.' The fronting of the object is a poetic inversion for emphasis.
segments.s753-g49: This is a compact clause: یکی (a certain/one) is an indefinite subject, خنجری تیز is the noun phrase 'a sharp dagger' (noun + adjective), and دادش is the past verb داد + enclitic object -ش ('gave it/to him'). چوآب is a simile 'like water' placed here to link to the following verb phrase; note the poetic fusion چوآب rather than separate چو آب.
segments.s753-g50: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (the subject — the dagger or the action — is implied). The following three adjectives/participles (کشنده, سبک, پرشتاب) describe that arrival: کشنده 'deadly/killing' (adjectival or participial), سبک 'light (in weight)', and پرشتاب 'very swift' (پر- intensifies شتاب). The sequence is terse/telegraphic, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s753-g51: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آن بدکنش is the demonstrative + agent 'that evildoer'. رفت is the past 'went', and نزدیک شاه is the locative complement 'near the king' placed after the verb, the normal order in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s753-g52: This clause is best read with ورا as the object pronoun 'him' (placed before the verb), دیده as the past/perfect participle 'seen' (passive sense 'was seen'), and پابند 'bound' describing his condition; در پیش گاه = 'in the presence (before the court/royal audience)'. The order object–participle–adjectival phrase is a poetic but grammatical passive-like construction.
segments.s753-g53: بلرزید is the past 'trembled/quake[d]' and appears before the subject خسرو (verb–subject order, common in poetry). چو introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when he saw him' (او را: object with the definite marker را before the verb; بدید is past 'saw').
segments.s753-g54: سرشکش is a small teardrop (subject), ز مژگان uses the ablative 'from the lashes/eyelashes', and به رخ برچکید is the directional/result phrase 'fell/sprang onto (his) face' — برچکید is a past verb meaning 'to begin to drip / to burst forth' describing the tear's motion onto the رخ (face).
segments.s753-g55: بدو گفت is 'to him he said' (بدو = to him/unto him, placed before the verb; گفت is the past 'said'). کای is the vocative 'O', followed by the adjective-noun phrase زشت نام تو چیست 'what is your vile/odious name' with چی 'what' and ست (ست = است) as the copula 'is' — the order adjective + noun + pronoun + copula is a poetic inversion.
segments.s753-g56: که introduces the explanatory/restrictive clause 'that/so that' (here giving the reason/force of the question). زاینده 'the begetter/one who gave life' is the object marked by را, بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'over you', and باید گریست is a modal construction 'one ought to weep' — باید (must/ought) + گریست, where گریست functions as the poetic/older infinitive/verb-stem 'to weep / to lament'.
segments.s753-g97: This clause states possession and number: the proper name (گرامی) + numeral phrase + the copula بود (past). Word order is Subject (name) — numeral phrase (ده و پنج فرزند = fifteen children) — بود (was/were). بود is the past-tense copula here marking that the subject had/there were that many; Persian often uses 'X ... بود' to assert existence/possession in past narrative.
segments.s753-g98: This is a locative relative clause describing where those children were: به ایوان شاه (to/in the king's iwan) followed by the relative/connector آنک and the adjectival predicate دربند بود. آنک introduces the following qualifying phrase 'who/which', دربند is an adjective/participial form meaning 'imprisoned/captive', and بود is again the past copula. Note that به here marks location/direction ('at/in'), and the relative phrase follows the noun it qualifies.
segments.s753-g99: به زندان + بکشتندشان is an action clause: به زندان ('into/at the prison') sets the location and بکشتندشان is the past-tense transitive verb with the enclitic object -شان 'them' (they killed them). بی‌گناه is an adverbial/ adjectival remark 'innocent' attached to the clause. The verb is active past (they slew them).
segments.s753-g100: بدانگه (thereupon/at that time) introduces the temporal clause; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/when ...'. برگشته شد is a verbal phrase: برگشته (past participle 'returned / turned back') + شد (past copula/become) yielding 'was turned/reversed'. بخت شاه = 'the king's fortune'. The whole clause states that at that moment the king's luck/fate changed.
segments.s753-g101: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'. چیزی نیارست here functions as a negative/result clause meaning 'obtained nothing' or 'gained nothing' (نیارست is a past-form verb used negatively); گفت closes the clause as narrative 'he said'. Word order is Subject — object/phrase — verb (typical Persian). Note: نیارست is an older/poetic form; its exact lexical senses given in the list did not match usage, so it is treated here as 'did not obtain/won'.
segments.s753-g102: همی‌داشت is a progressive/persistent past form 'he used to have / he was keeping'; آن انده is the object 'that little/that amount' (انده = 'small amount' or 'that bit' in context) and اندر نهفت is a compound verb phrase meaning 'kept hidden within' (اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hidden/concealed'). Together this says he had that small thing concealed.
segments.s753-g103: چو functions as the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'; شیرویه is the proper name of the person who heard; چندی گریست is an adverbial phrase 'wept for a while' (چندی = 'for some time / briefly', گریست = 'he cried/wailed'). The clause is a standard temporal + main-verb structure.
segments.s753-g104: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereafter'; نگهبان فرستاد is a transitive past clause 'he sent guards' — فرستاد is the past of 'to send' and (here) bi-specified quantity بیست follows as the object/amount 'twenty'. Word order places the temporal phrase first, then the verb phrase.
segments.s753-g81: سبک رفت is a short past clause where the adverbial adjective سبک (here 'lightly') precedes the past verb رفت ('he went/left'); Persian commonly places manner before the verb. جامه ازو در کشید follows as a second action: جامه ('garment') is the direct object, ازو ('from him') is the prepositional/oblique phrase placed before the verb, and کشید is the past verb 'pulled/drew/tore' (poetic use); the preposition/particle در appears fused here with the verb to indicate separation/tearing. The two clauses are simply coordinated by و 'and'.
segments.s753-g82: جگرگاه ('breast/innermost part') begins the clause as the object of the action; شاه جهان is a genitive/title phrase 'king of the world' (noun + appositive/epithet), and بر درید is the past verb درید 'tore' with the prepositional particle بر prefixed/placed before the verb to indicate force/over (literally 'rent upon'). Word order (object — possessor — verb) is typical poetic Persian where emphasis falls on the injured part.
segments.s753-g83: بپیچید is a past-form verb (پیچید 'turned/twisted') with the prefixed بـ appearing in classical/poetic Persian; it means 'it turned/whirled'. The conjunction و links the clauses. بر زد is a compound verb meaning 'struck upon' (بر + زد), and the noun phrase یکی سرد باد ('a certain cold wind') follows as the agent — the indefinite premodifier یکی + adjective سرد + noun باد.
segments.s753-g84: به زاری is an adverbial phrase 'with lamentation' (به + noun used adverbially). بران ('upon that' / 'thereon') points back to the previously mentioned garment; جامه is the object. بر جان بداد is a verbal phrase where بر جان ('upon life/soul') is the oblique target and بداد is the past verb (here used poetically as 'gave/laid/inflicted'); the whole phrase means 'laid/cast that garment upon his life' (i.e., in the context of death or mourning).
segments.s753-g85: برین گونه is a frozen locution meaning 'in this manner' (بر + این = برین + گونه 'way/manner'); گردد is the present/subjunctive/poetic third-person singular of گشت/گردیدن 'becomes/turns'. The repeated جهان جهان is a poetic duplication for emphasis ('the world — the world' / 'the world indeed'). The sentence order places the manner phrase first for emphasis, then the verb and the emphasized noun.
segments.s753-g86: همی is an emphatic/adverbial marker here meaning 'always' or 'continually'. راز خویش is 'its own secret' where خویش is split into خوی + ش (possessive clitic) in the manuscript; از تو دارد نهان is a light verb construction: از تو 'from you', دارد 'has/keeps' and نهان 'hidden' — together 'keeps hidden from you'. Word order places the object (راز خویش) before the verb دارد for emphasis.
segments.s753-g87: This is a string of nominal compounds/appositions: سخن‌سنج 'speech-judge/critic' modifies مرد 'man', with بی‌رنج‌گر ('harmless/untroubling') as another epithet; Persian allows multiple adjectives/compound nouns before the head noun. لاف 'boast' follows as the notion associated with such a man (poetic apposition). The segment lists qualities of the man rather than forming a full verbal clause.
segments.s753-g88: نبیند is the negative present/past-poetic form 'does not see' (ن- + بیند from دیدن), and ز کردار او 'from his deeds' gives the source/field of vision (z = 'from'). جز گزاف ('except/other than nonsense') is the exception phrase: جز 'except' + گزاف 'groundless/absurd' — together 'he sees nothing in his acts except absurdity/groundless boast'. The clause therefore states the man's judgment about deeds.
segments.s753-g41: بر زاد فرخ شد is a poetic word-order: بر زاد (literally “upon birth”) + فرخ (’fortunate’) + شد (’became/was’). Together they mean “was born fortunate.” The predicate phrase precedes the subject that follows; این مرد زشت is the subject phrase (این + noun + adjective) placed after the verbal predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s753-g42: که introduces a relative clause (“who/that”). هرگز + مبیناد yields a negated present/optative sense: literally “never appears/is seen,” i.e. “never will behold/see.” مبیناد is an older/poetic 3rd-person form (–ad ending) meaning “become apparent/appear.” خرم بهشت is the noun phrase “joyful/blissful paradise.”
segments.s753-g43: بدو گفت = “he said to him”: بدو is the prepositional pronoun ‘to him’ placed before the verb (typical in poetry), and گفت is the reporting verb in the past. کاین introduces the content clause (“that this …”). کارمنست is a possessive/predicative compound meaning “is my work” (کار + من + است collapsed to منست), an archaic/poetic concatenation meaning “it is my doing.”
segments.s753-g44: چو here functions temporally/conditionally “when/if.” سیرم is the adjective ‘sated’ with the enclitic -م (’me’/’I am sated’), and کنی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive of کردن ‘do/make’—together “when you make me sated / when you satisfy me.” The main clause این شکار منست follows: “this quarry/prey is mine,” where منست is an archaic contracted copula meaning ‘is mine.’
segments.s753-g45: بدو گفت again = “he said to him.” روگر is a compacted/poetic instruction to turn the face (literally a face‑turning expression); توانی + بکن is a conditional/optative sequence: توانی (’you can’) followed by the imperative/infinitival بکن ‘do it’—so “if you can, do (turn away).” Word order places the ability verb before the main verb for emphasis.
segments.s753-g46: وزین بیش = ‘and besides/more than this’ (و + زین + بیش in compact poetic form) introducing an additional admonition. مگشای is the prohibitive imperative (م‑ + گشای) meaning “do not open.” لب بر سخن literally “lip toward speech” = “open your lips to speak”; بر is the preposition indicating direction/result (‘toward’). The whole clause commands silence: “and furthermore do not open your lips to speech.”
segments.s753-g47: یکی کیسه دینار دادم تو را is straightforward: یکی ‘one / a certain’ + کیسه دینار ‘a purse of dinars’ as the direct object, then دادم (1st-person past “I gave”). تو را is the post-verbal object pronoun with the definite/object marker را used for emphasis (‘to you’). Poetic word order places the object phrase before the verb.
segments.s753-g48: چو here works as a comparative/simile ‘like/as.’ فرزند او یار دادم تو را is best read as “I gave you a companion as (if he were) his son” or “I gave you a companion like his son.” چو introduces the comparison, فرزند او (‘his son’) modifies the comparison, and again دادم تو را is the 1st‑person past ‘I gave you’ with تو را as emphasized object.
segments.s753-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برسم functions as a nominalized/possessive idea of 'my arrival' or 'my approach' (classical/poetic formation), and بدید is the past verb 'saw' (3rd sg.). اندر آمد is a compact verb phrase 'entered/came in' (در = in + آمد = came); بواژ is an adverbial/nominal 'in words' / 'uttering words' modifying the entrance. The sequence is chronological: when X saw my coming, he entered while speaking.
segments.s753-g74: نه + گاه = 'not a time' (negation + noun 'time'); سخن is 'speech/words' and بود is the past copula 'was' (here 'there was no time for speech'). و simply joins the clauses. گفتار ژاژ places گفتار 'utterance/speech' with the onomatopoetic adjective/nominal ژاژ ('shrill cry, clattering/babble') — i.e. the utterance took the form of a sharp cry. The clause negates the suitability of ordinary talk and characterizes the sound that was made.
segments.s753-g75: چو = 'when'. آن جامه‌ها را is the definite object phrase 'those garments' marked by را. بپوشید is the past action 'he put on / donned' (3rd sg. past); شاه is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb in poetic word order. The clause therefore states a past, punctual action: the king put on those clothes.
segments.s753-g76: به زمزم is a locative/benefactive phrase 'at/to Zamzam' (the sacred well); همی here is the classical preverb/particle giving immediacy or emphasis before the verb. توبه کرد is the two-word verb 'made repentance / repented' in past tense (کرد = did/made). از گناه is the standard prepositional complement 'from/of sin' specifying what he repented of. The whole clause describes a completed act of repentance at Zamzam.
segments.s753-g77: یکی is the indefinite 'one / someone / a certain (person)'. چادر نو is the noun + adjective 'new veil' (noun before adjective is common in poetry). به سر is the prepositional phrase 'on the head', and در کشید is the compound verbal idea 'drew over / pulled on' (کشید = pulled/drew — here used transitively with در as an adverbial/covering element meaning 'over'). The clause describes the physical action of drawing a veil over the head.
segments.s753-g78: بدان is instrumental/demonstrative 'by that / with that' (be + ān), تا here functions as a purposive/ resultive 'so that / thereby', and رخ جان ستان is a compound: رخ 'face' modified by جان‌ستان 'soul‑stealing' (literally 'life‑stealing'); راندید is the past verb 'drove away / banished'. The clause says that by that veil she drove away/veiled the life‑ (or soul‑) stealing face. (Note: دان and ستان appear here as parts of classical compounds; their individual senses in the supplied list do not line up exactly, so context yields the compound readings.)
segments.s753-g79: بشد here is the past/resultative 'it happened / thereupon he became/rose' (or 'then he was/rose' in narrative). مهر هرمزد is a divine/epic phrase: مهر (here a deity/name/divine force rather than ordinary 'love') qualified by هرمزد (Ahura Mazda). خنجر بدست is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'dagger in hand' (بدست = 'in the hand'). The whole unit gives a sudden, divine appearance with weapon in hand.
segments.s753-g80: در here is the noun 'door' (not the preposition), خانه پادشا is 'the king's house/palace' (noun + possessor), and راببست is the older fused form of را + بَبَست 'he shut/fastened' (past transitive). The verb closes the narrative clause: he fastened/shut the palace door.
segments.s753-g17: نییم is an archaic negative copula here (literally 'am not / are not') used to deny participation. اندرین کار = 'in this affair' (اندرین = in/among these + کار = affair/work). همداستان is an adjective meaning 'like-minded' or 'of the same counsel', modifying the implied participants.
segments.s753-g18: مزن is the prohibitive imperative/negative command of زدن used idiomatically with داستان to mean 'do not tell' or 'do not relate'. زین سپس is a poetic compound meaning 'from this / henceforth' (زین = زِ این, 'from this'), and پیش ما = 'before us / in our presence'. داستان = 'tale/story'.
segments.s753-g19: بترسید here functions as a past/inchoative sense 'was feared' or 'inspired fear' (classical usage where the verb expresses that fear occurred). شیروی is an epithet 'lion-like / heroic' modifying the subject; و connects to the next predicate; ترسنده is an adjective 'frightening/terrifying' and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s753-g20: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'for / because / in which'. در چنگ ایشان = 'in their grip/clutches' (چنگ used metaphorically as 'claw/grip'); یکی بنده بود = 'there was one servant' with یکی as an indefinite numeral 'one/a certain'. بود is the past copula marking existence in the clause.
segments.s753-g21: چنین داد پاسخ is a formulaic phrase 'he gave such an answer / answered thus' with داد as simple past of دادن and پاسخ as the object; که introduces the content of the answer. سرسوی دام = 'toward the trap' (سرسوی = toward; دام = trap).
segments.s753-g22: نیارد is an archaic negative verb form here meaning 'will not bring / will not put' (contracted classical form, effectively 'does not go/put'); مگر = 'except'; مردم = 'people'; زشت نام is a compound 'of ugly/bad name' i.e. 'of bad repute'. Together: '…will not go toward the trap except people of ill repute.'
segments.s753-g23: شما را places 'you' as the topical object (classical/pre-modern word order). سوی خانه = 'toward the house/home'; باید شدن is a semi-auxiliary periphrasis where باید = 'must/it is necessary' and شدن = 'to go/turn/become' — together 'you must go/return'.
segments.s753-g24: بران = 'on that/thereupon' (referring back to the previous action). آرزو رای is a noun cluster 'wish and counsel / desire and plan' (رای here = 'counsel, plan'); باید زدن uses باید = 'must' plus زدن as a light verb meaning 'to voice/express/strike out a plan' in this context (an idiomatic use of زدن with nouns), so 'a plan/wish must be put forward.'
segments.s753-g1: هر آنکس که introduces a free relative = "whoever/anyone who"; the clause then gives the action: بد کرد is a past simple verb phrase (کرد = did) with بد functioning as the moral/qualifying adjective 'evil/ill' describing the deed. با شهریار is a prepositional complement placed after the verb ('with the king').
segments.s753-g2: شب و روز is an adverbial collocation 'night and day'; ترسان is a predicative participle/adjective meaning 'afraid' describing the subject, and بد از روزگار is a prepositional phrase 'because of / fearing the fortunes of the world' (بد here characterizes the quality of those fortunes). The whole forms the predicate 'was terrified day and night, afraid because of fate.'
segments.s753-g3: چو here introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when'; شیروی is an adjectival/epithet 'lion-like' modifying an implied person, followed by two adjectives joined by و (ترسنده 'fearful' and خام 'naive/inexperienced'); بود is the past copula 'was', giving the state in the past.
segments.s753-g4: همان تخت is the subject 'that very throne'; پیش اندرش is a locative/positional phrase 'before him / in his presence' (پیش = before/at, اندر + ش = 'in/at his...'); دام بود is the predicate 'was a trap/snare' (دام = trap, بود = was). Note the clitic ش attached to اندر marks 'his'.
segments.s753-g5: The phrase is inverted but reads as subject + verb: اختر شمر 'Shamr's star' is the subject and بد انست (بدانست) is the past verb 'perceived/knew'; هرک دید is the subordinate relatival phrase 'whomever it saw' (هرک = whoever, دید = saw). The whole means the star perceived/realized whoever it looked upon.
segments.s753-g6: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; روز بزرگان is the subject phrase 'the day/time of the great (men)'; نخواهد + رسید is a classical future/negation construction meaning 'will not arrive' (نخواهد here functions as an auxiliary 'will not', paired with رسید 'arrive').
segments.s753-g7: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/they departed' placed before the relative clause for poetic inversion; هرکس که بد کرده بود is the relative clause 'each person who had done evil' with کرده بود forming the pluperfect ('had done').
segments.s753-g8: بدان کار is a prepositional phrase 'to/for that affair' (بدان = به + آن, classical 'to that'); تاب اندر آوردن is a compound meaning 'to bring endurance / to bear/endure' (تاب = endurance, اندر آوردن = to bring into), and آورده بود is the pluperfect 'had brought/possessed' — together: 'had had the endurance for that matter.'

sentences.s753-s17: From every side the king's ill-wisher kept searching; thus they saw a man on the road.
sentences.s753-s18: His two eyes were dark blue, and from privation he was yellow; a gaunt, hairy body and a lapis-blue face.
sentences.s753-s19: His feet were full of dust and his belly hungry; the body of the tyrannical man was naked.
sentences.s753-s20: No one in the world knew his name, either among the heavens or among the guests.
sentences.s753-s45: If you have treasure, or if you are eager and will not endure hardship, you will (not) remain in the house of sponge.
sentences.s753-s46: Choose harmlessness and honesty when you wish to obtain (it) from the Just Creator.
sentences.s753-s47: When news came to the bazaar and the road, that the king in that manner rose up ruined.
sentences.s753-s48: All the suspicious were put in prison; those needy ones were placed in that iwan.
sentences.s753-s29: "They call me Mehr‑Hormoz," he said; "I am a stranger in this city, friendless and without a mate."
sentences.s753-s30: Thus Khosrow said, when the time came into the hands of a base, suspicious man.
sentences.s753-s31: That very face did not remain among the people; in the world no one sought his affection.
sentences.s753-s32: A certain lowly fellow lay in wait before him; the little rogue thus said, 'O guide!'
sentences.s753-s5: They all at once left the court and went to Qobad, mentioning that act of injustice.
sentences.s753-s6: We have said it once, and this is another matter: for you there is no judgment in his mind except this one.
sentences.s753-s7: Two kings, both without offspring, sat in one city; one had the high place, the other the low.
sentences.s753-s8: When kinship between father and son increases, they cut off the heads of all the servants.
sentences.s753-s33: He set before him a basin of water and aromatics — musk and perfume — and a single clean robe, pleasing to the heart.
sentences.s753-s34: The worshipper heard his voice; the child did not know his secret.
sentences.s753-s35: The child's nurse came forward, and she carried a golden basin to the king.
sentences.s753-s36: With robe, a water‑attendant and water, Khosrow hurried to have them carried.
sentences.s753-s53: He arranged that their wives and children be cared for after the death of that slain king.
sentences.s753-s54: That kingship came into being, and there was so great an army, and men, and that court.
sentences.s753-s55: There was no one among those shahanshahs who had not been known among the renowned of former times.
sentences.s753-s56: One became so (poor) that he could not find ample bread and saw neither home nor palace.
sentences.s753-s57: The wise man says: whoever becomes safe from the dragon gains no value.
sentences.s753-s58: There is no tamer of the world except a brave hero like a whale — he will bite with his tooth when he seizes with his claw.
sentences.s753-s59: Now King Parviz's enterprise has come to an end: that famed throne, treasure, and army were made.
sentences.s753-s13: Search to find who in the world it is that has secretly brought this suffering upon us.
sentences.s753-s14: The king's ill-wisher kept searching for a killer, so that they might secretly destroy him.
sentences.s753-s15: No one in the world had that Zohreh, nor did anyone possess that same share of emeralds.
sentences.s753-s16: for he had spilled blood so kingly, and had hung whole mountains about his neck.
sentences.s753-s25: A certain man gave him a sharp dagger; it came like water — killing, light in weight, and very swift.
sentences.s753-s26: When that evildoer went near the king, they saw him bound before the court.
sentences.s753-s27: Khosrow trembled when he saw him; a teardrop from his lashes burst onto his face.
sentences.s753-s28: He said to him, 'O vile one, what is your name — that the one who gave you life should have to weep over you?'
sentences.s753-s49: Gerami had fifteen children, who were held in the king's iwan as captives.
sentences.s753-s50: They killed them (in) prison, though innocent; at that time the king's fortune was reversed.
sentences.s753-s51: The sovereign gained nothing and said that he had been keeping that small amount hidden.
sentences.s753-s52: When Shiruyeh heard it, he wept for a while; after that he sent twenty guards.
sentences.s753-s41: He moved lightly and pulled the garment from him; he tore open the king of the world's breast.
sentences.s753-s42: A cold wind whirled and struck; with lamentation it cast that garment upon his life.
sentences.s753-s43: In this way the world is made a world; the world ever keeps its own secret hidden from you.
sentences.s753-s44: A calm critic of speech, a harmless man, sees nothing in his deeds but empty boast.
sentences.s753-s21: This ugly man was born fortunate, who will never behold joyous paradise.
sentences.s753-s22: He said to him, “This battle is my business; when you satisfy me, this quarry is mine.”
sentences.s753-s23: He said to him, “Turn your face away if you can, and moreover do not open your lips to speak.”
sentences.s753-s24: I once gave you a purse of dinars; like his own son I gave you a companion.
sentences.s753-s37: When he saw my arrival he came in speaking; it was no time for words, and the utterance was a shrill cry.
sentences.s753-s38: When the king put on those garments, he repented at the Zamzam (well) for his sin.
sentences.s753-s39: A certain one drew a new veil over her head; with it she drove away that soul‑stealing face.
sentences.s753-s40: Then Mehr (the divine power) of Ahura Mazda, dagger in hand, arose and shut the king's house door.
sentences.s753-s9: We are not of this business of being like-minded; do not, from now on, tell the tale before us.
sentences.s753-s10: He inspired fear, was lion-like and terrifying, for one servant was in their clutches.
sentences.s753-s11: He answered thus: that no one would put his head toward the trap except people of bad repute.
sentences.s753-s12: You must go toward the house; and for that, wishes and counsel must be expressed.
sentences.s753-s1: Whoever did ill to the sovereign was terrified night and day, fearful of fate.
sentences.s753-s2: When a lion-like man was fearful and inexperienced, that very throne before him was a snare.
sentences.s753-s3: Shamr's star perceived—whomever it saw—that the day of the great would not come.
sentences.s753-s4: They departed—each one who had done wrong had borne up to that affair (had mustered the endurance for it).
